records.ety: [F. considrer, L. considerare, -sideratum, to consider, view attentively, prob. fr. con- + sidus, sideris, star, constellation; orig., therefore, to look at the stars. See Sidereal, and cf. Desire.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Considered (kn*sd"rd); p. pr. & vb. n. Considering.]

defs: To fix the mind on, with a view to a careful examination; to think on with care; to ponder; to study; to meditate on.
quotes: Thenceforth to speculations high or deep I turned my thoughts, and with capacious mind Considered all things visible.

defs: To look at attentively; to observe; to examine.
quotes: She considereth a field, and buyeth it.

defs: To have regard to; to take into view or account; to pay due attention to; to respect.
quotes: Consider, sir, the chance of war: the day Was yours by accident.
England could grow into a posture of being more united at home, and more considered abroad.

defs: To estimate; to think; to regard; to view.
quotes: Considered as plays, his works are absurd.
notes: ☞ The proper sense of consider is often blended with an idea of the result of considering; as, “Blessed is he that considereth the poor.” Ps. xli. 1.; i.e., considers with sympathy and pity. “Which [services] if I have not enough considered.” Shak.; i.e., requited as the sufficient considering of them would suggest. “Consider him liberally.” J. Hooker.

defs: To think seriously; to make examination; to reflect; to deliberate.
quotes: We will consider of your suit.
'T were to consider too curiously, to consider so.
She wished she had taken a moment to consider, before rushing down stairs.
